simple_explanation: Not healthy; suffering from a disease or illness.
daily_life_use: Used when feeling unwell, taking leave from work, or describing illness.
spoken_use: 'I feel sick.'
natural_native_use: I am calling in sick today.
examples: I can't come to school, I am sick.
The soup made me feel sick.
common_mistakes: Using 'sick' for long-term serious diseases (we usually use 'ill' for that, though 'sick' is acceptable).
hindi_confusion_correction: बीमार = sick. 'Ill' is more formal.
formal_informal_note: Informal and standard. 'Ill' is more formal.

simple_explanation: In need of sleep or rest; weary.
daily_life_use: Used after a long day of work or physical activity.
spoken_use: 'I am so tired.'
natural_native_use: I'm too tired to cook tonight.
examples: He was very tired after running the marathon.
I am tired of waiting for the bus.
common_mistakes: Saying 'tiring' instead of 'tired' to describe one's own feeling.
hindi_confusion_correction: मैं थका हुआ हूँ = I am tired. यह काम थका देने वाला है = This work is tiring.

simple_explanation: Lacking physical strength and energy.
daily_life_use: Used when recovering from illness, lifting heavy things, or feeling tired.
spoken_use: 'I feel very weak.'
natural_native_use: My legs feel weak after the long walk.
examples: The illness left him feeling very weak.
She was too weak to stand up.
common_mistakes: Confusing spelling with 'week' (à¤¸à¤ªà¥à¤¤à¤¾à¤¹).
hindi_confusion_correction: कमज़ोर = weak. सप्ताह = week. Both are pronounced exactly the same.

simple_explanation: Having great physical power and ability.
daily_life_use: Used regarding health, fitness, or moving heavy items.
spoken_use: 'You are very strong.'
natural_native_use: He has a strong immune system.
examples: You need to be strong to lift this box.
Eating healthy food makes you strong.
common_mistakes: None specifically common.
hindi_confusion_correction: मज़बूत = strong.

simple_explanation: Feeling good, pleased, or glad.
daily_life_use: I am so happy today because I passed my exam.
spoken_use: I'm really happy for you!
examples: She looks very happy.
Are you happy with the result?
common_mistakes: Saying 'I have happy' instead of 'I am happy'.
hindi_confusion_correction: In Hindi we say 'मुझे खुशी है' (I have happiness), but in English use 'I am happy'.
formal_informal_note: Used in all contexts. 'Delighted' is more formal.

simple_explanation: Feeling unhappy or sorrowful.
daily_life_use: He was sad when his pet died.
spoken_use: Why are you looking so sad?
natural_native_use: I'm feeling a bit down today.
examples: It's a sad story.
Don't be sad, things will get better.
common_mistakes: Saying 'sad from' instead of 'sad about'.
hindi_confusion_correction: 'From' (से) is often wrongly translated literally. Use 'about' for feelings related to news.

simple_explanation: Having a strong feeling of displeasure.
daily_life_use: My father was angry when I broke the glass.
spoken_use: Please don't get angry at me.
examples: She gave him an angry look.
Why are you so angry?
common_mistakes: Saying 'angry on' instead of 'angry at' or 'angry with'.
hindi_confusion_correction: In Hindi we say 'मुझ पर गुस्सा' (angry on me), but English uses 'angry at' or 'angry with'.

simple_explanation: Feeling very happy and enthusiastic about something.
daily_life_use: I am excited about my upcoming trip to Goa.
spoken_use: Are you excited for the party?
natural_native_use: I can't wait for the trip!
examples: The kids are excited to go to the zoo.
She was too excited to sleep.
common_mistakes: Using 'exciting' instead of 'excited' to describe one's own feelings.
hindi_confusion_correction: 'Excited' is how you feel. 'Exciting' is the thing causing the feeling (e.g., an exciting movie).

simple_explanation: Feeling worried or slightly afraid about what might happen.
daily_life_use: I always get nervous before an interview.
spoken_use: I'm a bit nervous about this test.
natural_native_use: I have butterflies in my stomach.
examples: He looked very nervous.
Don't be nervous, you'll do great.
common_mistakes: Saying 'I am feeling nervousness' instead of 'I am feeling nervous'.
hindi_confusion_correction: Use the adjective 'nervous' to describe your state, not the noun 'nervousness'.

simple_explanation: Feeling afraid or fearful.
daily_life_use: She is scared of dogs.
spoken_use: Are you scared?
natural_native_use: That scared the life out of me!
examples: I was too scared to move.
He's scared of heights.
common_mistakes: Saying 'scared from' instead of 'scared of'.
hindi_confusion_correction: In Hindi we say 'कुत्ते से डर लगता है' (scared from dogs), but English uses 'scared of'.
formal_informal_note: More informal. 'Afraid' or 'frightened' can be slightly more formal.

simple_explanation: Unable to understand or think clearly.
daily_life_use: I am confused by these instructions.
spoken_use: I'm a little confused, can you repeat that?
examples: The old man looked confused.
Are you confused about what to do?
common_mistakes: Using 'confusing' to describe oneself (e.g., I am confusing) instead of 'confused'.
hindi_confusion_correction: You are 'confused' (उलझन में). The topic is 'confusing' (उलझाने वाला).

simple_explanation: In need of rest or sleep.
daily_life_use: I am very tired after working all day.
spoken_use: I'm too tired to go out tonight.
examples: My legs are tired.
I'm tired of eating the same food.
common_mistakes: Saying 'I am tire' instead of 'I am tired'.
hindi_confusion_correction: Make sure to add the 'd' at the end; 'tire' is the rubber wheel on a car.
formal_informal_note: Used in all contexts. 'Exhausted' is stronger.

simple_explanation: Feeling unhappy because something is not interesting.
daily_life_use: I got bored during the long meeting.
spoken_use: I'm so bored, let's do something fun.
examples: Are you bored with this game?
The children quickly got bored.
common_mistakes: Saying 'I am boring' instead of 'I am bored'.
hindi_confusion_correction: 'I am boring' means you make other people bored. 'I am bored' means you feel dull.

simple_explanation: Feeling or showing wonder because of something unexpected.
daily_life_use: I was surprised when I saw him at the mall.
spoken_use: I'm really surprised by this gift!
examples: She gave a surprised cry.
We were surprised to hear the news.
common_mistakes: Using 'surprising' to describe oneself instead of 'surprised'.
hindi_confusion_correction: You are 'surprised' (हैरान). The news is 'surprising' (हैरान करने वाली).

simple_explanation: Feeling very pleased and satisfied about something you or someone else has done.
daily_life_use: My parents are very proud of me.
spoken_use: I'm so proud of what you've achieved.
natural_native_use: You should be proud of yourself.
examples: He is proud of his new car.
She wore a proud smile.
common_mistakes: Saying 'proud on' instead of 'proud of'.
hindi_confusion_correction: In Hindi we say 'मुझ पर गर्व है' (proud on me), but in English it is 'proud of me'.

simple_explanation: Feeling upset because someone has something you want.
daily_life_use: He was jealous of his brother's success.
spoken_use: Don't be jealous, your time will come.
examples: Why are you so jealous of her?
A jealous friend is not a true friend.
common_mistakes: Saying 'jealous from' instead of 'jealous of'.
hindi_confusion_correction: In Hindi 'उससे जलता है' (jealous from him), but English uses 'jealous of'.

simple_explanation: Experiencing worry, unease, or nervousness.
daily_life_use: She felt anxious before her medical test.
spoken_use: I'm a little anxious about tomorrow's presentation.
examples: He gave an anxious look.
They are anxious for her safety.
common_mistakes: Confusing 'anxious' with 'eager' (e.g., 'I am anxious to meet you' when you mean excited).
hindi_confusion_correction: Anxious carries a tone of worry. If you are just excited, use 'eager'.

simple_explanation: Not showing or feeling nervousness, anger, or other strong emotions.
daily_life_use: You need to stay calm during an emergency.
spoken_use: Just try to keep calm.
natural_native_use: Take a deep breath and chill out.
examples: She has a calm voice.
The sea is calm today.
common_mistakes: Pronouncing the 'l' in calm.
hindi_confusion_correction: The 'l' is silent. It's pronounced 'kahm', not 'kalm'.

hindi_meaning: निराश और क्रोधित / कुंठित (Niraash aur krodhit / Kunthit)
simple_explanation: Feeling annoyed or less confident because you cannot achieve what you want.
daily_life_use: I feel frustrated when my computer runs slow.
spoken_use: I'm getting really frustrated with this math problem.
examples: He became frustrated with the lack of progress.
A frustrated sigh.
common_mistakes: Saying 'I am frustrating' instead of 'I am frustrated'.
hindi_confusion_correction: You are 'frustrated'. The computer is 'frustrating'.
formal_informal_note: Used widely in both formal and informal contexts.

simple_explanation: Sad because one has no friends or company.
daily_life_use: He felt very lonely when he moved to a new city.
spoken_use: Do you ever get lonely living by yourself?
natural_native_use: I feel a bit left out.
examples: She lives a lonely life.
The house feels lonely without you.
common_mistakes: Confusing 'lonely' with 'alone'.
hindi_confusion_correction: 'Alone' means nobody is with you physically. 'Lonely' means you feel sad because nobody is with you.

hindi_meaning: दोषी / अपराधी महसूस करना (Doshi / Apraadhi mahsoos karna)
simple_explanation: Feeling bad because you have done something wrong.
daily_life_use: I feel guilty for eating all the cake.
spoken_use: Don't make me feel guilty about it!
natural_native_use: I feel bad about what I did.
examples: He gave a guilty smile.
She felt guilty about leaving him behind.
common_mistakes: Saying 'guilty for' instead of 'guilty about' in some contexts.
hindi_confusion_correction: Use 'feel guilty about [something]' to express your emotion clearly.
formal_informal_note: Common in all contexts. Has legal meaning as well.

simple_explanation: Surprised and upset.
daily_life_use: I was shocked when I heard the bad news.
spoken_use: I'm completely shocked!
examples: The shocked crowd stared in disbelief.
He was shocked to see her there.
common_mistakes: Pronouncing it as 'shock-ed' with two syllables.
hindi_confusion_correction: It is a one-syllable word ending in a 't' sound: 'shahkt'.

simple_explanation: Free from tension and anxiety.
daily_life_use: I feel very relaxed after a long sleep.
spoken_use: You look completely relaxed today.
examples: He has a very relaxed attitude.
A relaxed atmosphere.
common_mistakes: Saying 'I am relax' instead of 'I am relaxed'.
hindi_confusion_correction: Remember to use the past participle form 'relaxed' as an adjective.

simple_explanation: Slightly angry or irritated.
daily_life_use: I get annoyed when people talk loudly on the phone.
spoken_use: I'm really annoyed with him right now.
examples: She gave him an annoyed glance.
Are you annoyed with me?
common_mistakes: Confusing 'annoyed' and 'annoying'.
hindi_confusion_correction: You are 'annoyed' (चिढ़े हुए). The loud noise is 'annoying' (चिढ़ाने वाला).

simple_explanation: Feeling or inspiring optimism about a future event.
daily_life_use: I am hopeful that I will get the job.
spoken_use: I'm feeling hopeful about tomorrow.
examples: She is hopeful for a better future.
He gave a hopeful smile.
common_mistakes: Adding '-ly' to mean the state (saying 'I feel hopefully' instead of 'I feel hopeful').
hindi_confusion_correction: Use the adjective 'hopeful' after verbs like 'feel' or 'am'.

simple_explanation: Sad or displeased because someone or something has failed to fulfill one's hopes or expectations.
daily_life_use: I was disappointed with my low test scores.
spoken_use: I'm a bit disappointed that you couldn't come.
examples: He looked disappointed.
We are disappointed by the delay.
common_mistakes: Saying 'disappointed from' instead of 'disappointed with' or 'disappointed in'.
hindi_confusion_correction: In Hindi 'तुमसे निराश हूँ' translates conceptually to 'disappointed in you' or 'with you', not 'from you'.

simple_explanation: Feeling ashamed or self-conscious.
daily_life_use: He felt embarrassed when he forgot her name.
spoken_use: I was so embarrassed, I wanted the ground to swallow me up.
examples: She gave an embarrassed laugh.
There's no need to be embarrassed.
common_mistakes: Spelling it with one 'r' or one 's' (embarased).
hindi_confusion_correction: Remember: double 'r' and double 's'. And don't use 'embarrassing' to describe how you feel.

simple_explanation: Feeling or showing appreciation for something done or received.
daily_life_use: I am grateful for all your help.
spoken_use: I'm really grateful to you.
examples: We are grateful for your support.
She gave a grateful smile.
common_mistakes: Spelling it as 'greatful'.
hindi_confusion_correction: It comes from 'gratitude', not 'great'. So it is spelled 'grateful'.
formal_informal_note: Slightly formal, but widely used. 'Thankful' is very similar.

simple_explanation: Having a strong emotional effect that is too much to handle.
daily_life_use: I am feeling overwhelmed with all this homework.
spoken_use: It's all just a bit overwhelming right now.
natural_native_use: I've got too much on my plate.
examples: She was overwhelmed by the love of her fans.
I am overwhelmed with work.
common_mistakes: Using it only negatively. It can also be positive (e.g., overwhelmed with joy).
hindi_confusion_correction: It can mean 'दबा हुआ' (burdened) or 'भावविभोर' (deeply moved) depending on context.

simple_explanation: Experiencing mental or emotional pressure.
daily_life_use: I am feeling very stressed because of my exams.
spoken_use: I'm super stressed right now.
examples: He looks very stressed.
Don't get stressed over small things.
common_mistakes: Saying 'I am taking stress' instead of 'I am stressed'.
hindi_confusion_correction: In Hindi we say 'मैं स्ट्रेस ले रहा हूँ', but in English we say 'I am stressed' or 'I am stressing out'.

simple_explanation: Thinking about unpleasant things that have happened or might happen.
daily_life_use: My mother is worried about my health.
spoken_use: I'm really worried about him.
examples: You look worried, what's wrong?
There is nothing to be worried about.
common_mistakes: Saying 'worried for' instead of 'worried about' in most cases.
hindi_confusion_correction: Use 'about' to indicate what is causing the concern.

simple_explanation: Feeling sure of yourself and your abilities.
daily_life_use: She is confident that she will win the race.
spoken_use: I feel pretty confident about this.
natural_native_use: I know I can do it.
examples: He is a confident public speaker.
Are you confident in your answer?
common_mistakes: Using the noun 'confidence' instead of the adjective 'confident' (e.g., I am very confidence).
hindi_confusion_correction: You *have* confidence (noun). You *are* confident (adjective).

simple_explanation: No longer feeling distressed or anxious.
daily_life_use: I felt relieved when I found my lost keys.
spoken_use: Phew, I'm so relieved to hear that!
natural_native_use: That's a weight off my shoulders.
examples: He gave a relieved sigh.
She was relieved that the test was over.
common_mistakes: Using 'relief' (noun) instead of 'relieved' (adjective) like 'I am very relief'.
hindi_confusion_correction: You feel 'relieved'. 'Relief' is the noun form (e.g., What a relief!).

simple_explanation: Feeling emotional pain or sadness.
daily_life_use: I was very hurt by her harsh words.
spoken_use: I'm really hurt that you didn't invite me.
examples: He looked angry and hurt.
Are you hurt by what I said?
common_mistakes: Using 'hurted'. The past tense and participle of hurt is just 'hurt'.
hindi_confusion_correction: Never say 'She hurted me'. Say 'She hurt me'.

simple_explanation: Eager to know or learn something.
daily_life_use: I am curious to know what is inside the box.
spoken_use: I'm just curious, how old are you?
examples: Children are always very curious.
She gave him a curious glance.
common_mistakes: Saying 'curious for' instead of 'curious about'.
hindi_confusion_correction: In English, we say 'curious about' something.

simple_explanation: Greatly surprised; astonished.
daily_life_use: I was amazed by the beautiful painting.
spoken_use: I'm just amazed at how fast you finished that.
examples: He stood there amazed.
We were amazed to see him.
common_mistakes: Confusing 'amazed' (how you feel) with 'amazing' (the thing causing the feeling).
hindi_confusion_correction: You are 'amazed'. The painting is 'amazing'.

simple_explanation: In a state of general unhappiness or despondency.
daily_life_use: He has been feeling depressed since he lost his job.
spoken_use: I'm feeling really depressed lately.
examples: She looked very depressed.
The long winter makes me depressed.
common_mistakes: Using it lightly for minor sadness instead of using 'sad' or 'upset'.
hindi_confusion_correction: Depressed is a very strong word and is also a medical condition. Use 'sad' for temporary feelings.
formal_informal_note: Can be both a casual expression of sadness and a formal medical term.

simple_explanation: Unhappy, disappointed, or worried.
daily_life_use: She was upset because her friend lied to her.
spoken_use: Are you upset with me?
examples: He got upset when he heard the news.
Don't be upset.
common_mistakes: Saying 'I am upset from you' instead of 'I am upset with you'.
hindi_confusion_correction: In Hindi 'तुमसे' is 'from you', but English uses 'with you' for people.

simple_explanation: Extremely happy and excited.
daily_life_use: I was thrilled to get the first prize.
spoken_use: I'm absolutely thrilled for you!
examples: We are thrilled with the new house.
She was thrilled by the invitation.
common_mistakes: Using it for mild happiness; it should only be used for extreme happiness.
hindi_confusion_correction: 'Thrilled' is much stronger than 'खुश' (happy). It's closer to 'रोमांचित'.
formal_informal_note: Common in both formal and informal contexts.

simple_explanation: Finding something funny or entertaining.
daily_life_use: The children were amused by the clown's tricks.
spoken_use: I wasn't really amused by his joke.
natural_native_use: That gave me a good chuckle.
examples: She gave an amused smile.
We were all amused at his story.
common_mistakes: Confusing 'amused' with 'amazing'.
hindi_confusion_correction: 'Amused' means you find it funny/entertaining. 'Amazing' means wonderful/surprising.
formal_informal_note: Slightly formal but used in everyday speech.

hindi_meaning: बुरी तरह थका हुआ (Buri tarah thaka hua)
simple_explanation: Very tired.
daily_life_use: I was exhausted after running the marathon.
spoken_use: I'm completely exhausted, I need to sleep.
examples: The exhausted workers went home.
She felt exhausted but happy.
common_mistakes: Saying 'very exhausted'. It's better to say 'completely exhausted'.
hindi_confusion_correction: 'Exhausted' already means 'very tired', so 'very exhausted' is redundant.
meaning_hi: बुरी तरह थका हुआ (Buri tarah thaka hua)

simple_explanation: Feeling sudden, overwhelming fear or terror.
daily_life_use: She panicked when she couldn't find her passport.
spoken_use: I panicked and completely forgot what to say.
examples: The panicked crowd ran towards the exit.
He sounded panicked on the phone.
common_mistakes: Spelling it as 'paniced'.
hindi_confusion_correction: When adding '-ed' to panic, you must add a 'k': panicked.

simple_explanation: Content or pleased with what has been done or what has happened.
daily_life_use: I am satisfied with the results of the test.
spoken_use: Are you satisfied now?
examples: He gave a satisfied smile.
We are satisfied with their service.
common_mistakes: Saying 'satisfied from' instead of 'satisfied with'.
hindi_confusion_correction: In Hindi we say 'इससे संतुष्ट' (satisfied from this), but English uses 'with'.

simple_explanation: Wretchedly unhappy or uncomfortable.
daily_life_use: He has been feeling miserable due to his heavy cold.
spoken_use: I feel absolutely miserable today.
examples: She had a miserable time at the party.
The wet weather made everyone miserable.
common_mistakes: Mispronouncing the word with four hard syllables (mi-ser-a-ble).
hindi_confusion_correction: It is usually pronounced smoothly with three syllables: MIZ-ruh-bul.

simple_explanation: In accordance with fact or reality.
daily_life_use: Agreeing that a statement is a fact.
spoken_use: Is that a true story?
examples: It is true that I am moving.
Her dream came true.
common_mistakes: Saying 'speak the true' instead of 'speak the truth'.
hindi_confusion_correction: Truth is the noun (सच्चाई), True is the adjective (सच).

simple_explanation: Not true or not real.
daily_life_use: Answering test questions or describing lies.
spoken_use: That statement is false.
examples: He gave a false name.
The rumors were false.
common_mistakes: Confusing 'false' with 'fake' (fake means an imitation, false means not true).
hindi_confusion_correction: False is mostly used for facts. Fake is used for objects or people.

simple_explanation: To be desired or approved of; of high quality.
daily_life_use: Complimenting something or saying how you feel.
spoken_use: This food is very good.
natural_native_use: I'm good. (Meaning I don't need anything/I'm fine)
examples: He is a good person.
Did you have a good time?
common_mistakes: Saying 'he plays football good' instead of 'he plays football well'.
hindi_confusion_correction: Good is an adjective (good boy). Well is an adverb (plays well).

simple_explanation: Of poor quality or low standard; unacceptable.
daily_life_use: Describing something you didn't like or a negative situation.
spoken_use: That is a bad idea.
examples: The weather is very bad today.
I feel bad about what happened.
common_mistakes: Saying 'I feel badly'. You should say 'I feel bad'.
hindi_confusion_correction: Can mean character (बुरा) or quality (ख़राब).

simple_explanation: Extremely good or impressive.
daily_life_use: Praising a person, event, or thing enthusiastically.
spoken_use: The concert was awesome!
examples: You did an awesome job.
That's an awesome shirt.
common_mistakes: Overusing it for mundane things (like 'this water is awesome').
hindi_confusion_correction: Equivalent to 'कमाल का' or 'ज़बरदस्त'.

simple_explanation: Very bad or unpleasant.
daily_life_use: Complaining about something terrible.
spoken_use: The smell is awful.
examples: He made an awful mistake.
The weather was awful.
common_mistakes: Confusing 'awful' (very bad) with 'awesome' (very good).
hindi_confusion_correction: Used like 'बहुत ही बकवास' or 'भयानक'.

simple_explanation: Above average, very good, or large.
daily_life_use: Showing excitement or praising someone's idea.
spoken_use: That's a great idea!
natural_native_use: Great! (Can be used sarcastically when something goes wrong)
examples: We had a great time at the party.
She is a great leader.
common_mistakes: Confusing the spelling with 'grate' (like a cheese grater).
hindi_confusion_correction: Often just means 'बहुत अच्छा' in daily conversation, not necessarily 'महान'.

simple_explanation: Extremely bad or serious.
daily_life_use: Describing pain, bad experiences, or bad news.
spoken_use: I have a terrible headache.
examples: The food was terrible.
I feel terrible for forgetting your birthday.
common_mistakes: Spelling it as 'terable' or 'turrible'.
hindi_confusion_correction: Similar to 'awful' but often used more for pain and sadness.

simple_explanation: Feeling or showing pleasure or contentment.
daily_life_use: Expressing a good mood or celebrating.
spoken_use: I am very happy today.
examples: She looks happy.
Money can't buy happy endings.
common_mistakes: Saying 'I am very much happy' instead of 'I am very happy'.
hindi_confusion_correction: Very straightforward.

simple_explanation: Feeling or showing sorrow; unhappy.
daily_life_use: Expressing grief or reacting to bad news.
spoken_use: Why are you sad?
examples: I felt sad when she left.
It is a sad movie.
common_mistakes: Using 'sad' to describe physical pain. Sad is only for emotions.
hindi_confusion_correction: Translation is exact. उदास = sad.

simple_explanation: Having a strong feeling of annoyance or hostility.
daily_life_use: Expressing frustration or warning someone.
spoken_use: I am very angry with him.
examples: Please don't be angry.
The customer was very angry.
common_mistakes: Saying 'I am anger'. Anger is the noun, angry is the adjective.
hindi_confusion_correction: In Hindi, 'मुझे गुस्सा आ रहा है', in English 'I am getting angry'.

simple_explanation: In need of sleep or rest.
daily_life_use: Explaining why you want to go to bed or stop working.
spoken_use: I am too tired to cook.
examples: She was tired after a long day.
I am tired of waiting.
common_mistakes: Confusing 'tired' with 'tiring'. A long walk is tiring; you feel tired.
hindi_confusion_correction: Tired of something means 'ऊब जाना' or 'पक जाना' (I am tired of this job).

simple_explanation: Feeling a need or desire for food.
daily_life_use: Asking for food or suggesting meal times.
spoken_use: I am hungry, let's eat.
examples: Are you hungry yet?
The baby is crying because he is hungry.
common_mistakes: Saying 'I have hunger' (literal translation from some languages). Say 'I am hungry'.
hindi_confusion_correction: Exact match for 'भूख लगना' (I am getting hungry).

simple_explanation: Feeling a need to drink liquid.
daily_life_use: Asking for water.
spoken_use: I am thirsty, can I get some water?
examples: Running makes me thirsty.
The dog was very thirsty.
common_mistakes: Pronouncing 'thirsty' as 'tarsty'. Ensure the 'th' sound is made with the tongue on the teeth.
hindi_confusion_correction: Exact match for 'प्यास लगना'.

simple_explanation: Having a great deal to do; occupied.
daily_life_use: Declining an invitation because you have work.
spoken_use: I am very busy today.
examples: The manager is busy right now.
It was a busy week.
common_mistakes: Saying 'I am busying'. Busy is an adjective, not a verb.
hindi_confusion_correction: Can refer to a person (व्यस्त) or a place with a lot of people/traffic (भीड़-भाड़).

simple_explanation: Not costing money, or not busy (having available time).
daily_life_use: Asking if someone is available, or checking if something costs money.
spoken_use: Are you free tonight?
natural_native_use: For free. / I've got some free time.
examples: The tickets are free.
I will call you when I am free.
common_mistakes: Using 'free' for an empty seat (use 'available' or 'empty').
hindi_confusion_correction: Covers both 'मुफ़्त' (no cost) and 'खाली' (time).

simple_explanation: Prepared to do something or for something to happen.
daily_life_use: Checking if someone is prepared to leave or start an activity.
spoken_use: Are you ready to go?
examples: Dinner is ready.
I am getting ready for the party.
common_mistakes: Saying 'I am readying' (get ready is the verb phrase).
hindi_confusion_correction: Exact match for 'तैयार'.

simple_explanation: Achieved without great effort; presenting few difficulties.
daily_life_use: Describing a simple task or test.
spoken_use: The exam was very easy.
examples: English is easy to learn.
It's not as easy as it looks.
common_mistakes: Saying 'more easy'. The comparative is 'easier'.
hindi_confusion_correction: Take it easy means 'आराम से' or 'शांत रहो'.

simple_explanation: Feeling regret or apologizing.
daily_life_use: When you make a mistake.
spoken_use: I am sorry.
examples: I am sorry for being late.
Sorry, I didn't hear you.
common_mistakes: Saying 'sorry' too much.
hindi_confusion_correction: In Hindi 'maaf karna' can be heavy, 'sorry' is used even for small things.

simple_explanation: Of high quality or standard.
daily_life_use: Describing something you like or approve of.
spoken_use: This is a good book.
examples: Have a good day.
The food was really good.
common_mistakes: Using 'good' instead of 'well' (e.g., I did good vs I did well).
hindi_confusion_correction: Hindi 'achha' has many uses; 'good' is strictly an adjective.
formal_informal_note: Good is standard; excellent/superb are more formal.

simple_explanation: Of poor quality or low standard.
daily_life_use: Describing something you dislike.
spoken_use: This is a bad idea.
examples: I had a bad day.
The weather is very bad.
common_mistakes: Confusing 'bad' with 'bed' in pronunciation.
hindi_confusion_correction: In Hindi 'kharaab' is used for broken things, 'bad' is more for quality/behavior.
formal_informal_note: Bad is standard; poor/unacceptable are formal.

simple_explanation: Costing very little; relatively low in price.
daily_life_use: Shopping for bargains.
spoken_use: This shirt is very cheap.
natural_native_use: Do you have a cheaper one?
examples: I bought a cheap watch.
It is cheaper to travel by bus.
common_mistakes: Using 'cheap' for people can mean they are mean with money.
hindi_confusion_correction: Hindi 'sasta' is cheap.

simple_explanation: Costing a lot of money.
daily_life_use: Describing costly items.
spoken_use: This car is too expensive.
examples: Gold is expensive.
I cannot buy it, it is too expensive.
common_mistakes: Misspelling as 'expencive'.
hindi_confusion_correction: Hindi 'mahanga' is expensive.

simple_explanation: Having a good, generous, and friendly nature.
daily_life_use: Used to describe someone who helps others.
spoken_use: It is very kind of you to help.
natural_native_use: She has a very kind heart.
examples: Always be kind to animals.
He gave me a kind smile.
common_mistakes: Saying 'He is kind person' instead of 'He is a kind person'.
hindi_confusion_correction: 'Kind' means both 'दयालु' (adjective) and 'प्रकार' (noun, like 'what kind?').

simple_explanation: Not polite; offensive or bad-mannered.
daily_life_use: Used when someone talks back badly or lacks manners.
spoken_use: Why are you being so rude?
natural_native_use: It's rude to point at people.
examples: The waiter was very rude to us.
I didn't mean to be rude.
common_mistakes: Using 'rude' for someone who is just angry. Rude specifically means lacking manners.
hindi_confusion_correction: Rude translates exactly to 'बदतमीज़'.
formal_informal_note: Used in all contexts to criticize behavior.

simple_explanation: Showing good manners and respect for others.
daily_life_use: Used to praise children or describe professional behavior.
spoken_use: He is a very polite young man.
natural_native_use: Please try to be polite to the guests.
examples: It is polite to say 'thank you'.
She gave a polite refusal.
common_mistakes: Saying 'He has polite' instead of 'He is polite'.
hindi_confusion_correction: Polite is an adjective (विनम्र). The noun form is 'politeness' (विनम्रता).
formal_informal_note: A highly valued trait in formal settings.

simple_explanation: Believing that you are better or more important than other people.
daily_life_use: Used to describe someone who shows off and looks down on others.
spoken_use: He is too arrogant to admit his mistake.
natural_native_use: I can't stand her arrogant attitude.
examples: The actor came across as very arrogant.
Don't be so arrogant just because you won.
common_mistakes: Confusing 'arrogant' with 'confident'. Confidence is positive, arrogance is negative.
hindi_confusion_correction: 'Proud' can be good (मुझे तुम पर गर्व है), but 'arrogant' is always bad (घमंडी).

simple_explanation: Kind and pleasant towards others.
daily_life_use: Used to describe someone easy to talk to.
spoken_use: Everyone in this town is so friendly.
natural_native_use: She gave me a friendly smile.
examples: Our new neighbors are very friendly.
He has a friendly nature.
common_mistakes: Using 'friendly' as an adverb. It is an adjective. Say 'in a friendly way'.
hindi_confusion_correction: Friendly describes a person's nature, not just if they are your friend.

simple_explanation: Nervous or uncomfortable around other people.
daily_life_use: Used to describe kids who hide behind parents or adults who don't talk much.
spoken_use: Don't be shy, come and say hello.
natural_native_use: He is too shy to speak in public.
examples: She was a very shy child.
I felt shy when everyone looked at me.
common_mistakes: Pronouncing it as 'she'. It rhymes with 'my'.
hindi_confusion_correction: Shy is a personality trait. If you are embarrassed by a mistake, use 'embarrassed', not 'shy'.

simple_explanation: Fond of talking a lot.
daily_life_use: Used for someone who chatters continuously.
spoken_use: She is a very talkative person.
natural_native_use: He becomes talkative when he's nervous.
examples: I sat next to a talkative passenger on the train.
My daughter is very talkative.
common_mistakes: Saying 'He is too much talker' instead of 'He is talkative'.
hindi_confusion_correction: Exact match for 'बातूनी'. Another word is 'chatty'.
formal_informal_note: Informal. For a formal negative sense, you might use 'loquacious'.

simple_explanation: Making little or no noise; a person who speaks very little.
daily_life_use: Used to tell someone to stop talking or to describe a calm person.
spoken_use: Please be quiet in the library.
examples: The class was surprisingly quiet.
She is quiet, but very smart.
common_mistakes: Spelling it as 'quite' (which means 'very'). Remember: q-u-i-E-t.
hindi_confusion_correction: 'Quiet' can mean both no sound (चुप) and a calm personality (शांत स्वभाव).

simple_explanation: Intelligent, or showing quick and clever thinking.
daily_life_use: Used to compliment someone's intelligence or their clothing.
spoken_use: That was a smart move.
natural_native_use: She is a very smart cookie.
examples: He is the smartest student in the class.
You look very smart in that suit.
common_mistakes: Using 'smart' only for appearance (looking good). In modern English, it mostly means intelligent.
hindi_confusion_correction: In India, 'smart' often means good-looking. Native speakers usually use it for 'intelligent'.

simple_explanation: Unwilling to work or use energy.
daily_life_use: Used when someone avoids doing their chores or duties.
spoken_use: Don't be so lazy, get up and help!
natural_native_use: I'm feeling a bit lazy today.
examples: He is too lazy to cook.
We had a lazy afternoon at the beach.
common_mistakes: Saying 'He is feeling laziness' instead of 'He is feeling lazy'.
hindi_confusion_correction: A perfect translation for 'आलसी'.
formal_informal_note: Can be an insult or just a casual description of a mood.

simple_explanation: Putting a lot of effort and care into work.
daily_life_use: Used to praise someone's dedication to their job or studies.
spoken_use: She is a hardworking student.
examples: My father is a very hardworking man.
Hardworking employees usually get promoted.
common_mistakes: Spelling it as two words 'hard working'. It's usually one word.
hindi_confusion_correction: Means the same as 'मेहनती'.
formal_informal_note: Excellent word to use in job interviews or formal recommendations.

simple_explanation: Telling the truth and not stealing or cheating.
daily_life_use: Used to describe trustworthy people or true statements.
spoken_use: To be honest, I didn't like the food.
examples: Give me your honest opinion.
She is honest about her past.
common_mistakes: Pronouncing the 'H'. The 'H' is silent! Say 'On-ist', not 'Hon-ist'.
hindi_confusion_correction: Using 'an honest' is correct because it starts with an 'O' sound. (He is an honest boy).
formal_informal_note: Common in both formal and casual English.

simple_explanation: Not genuine; pretending to be something one is not.
daily_life_use: Used for counterfiet items, but also for people who act nice but aren't.
spoken_use: I don't like her, she's so fake.
examples: She has a lot of fake friends.
He was using a fake ID.
common_mistakes: Using 'duplicate' for a person. Always use 'fake' for an ungenuine person.
hindi_confusion_correction: In Hindi 'नकली' means a fake item. For people, it means two-faced (दोगला/दिखावटी).
formal_informal_note: Informal when used for people's personalities.

simple_explanation: Giving firm and constant support or allegiance to a person or institution.
daily_life_use: Used for good friends, long-term employees, or pets like dogs.
spoken_use: He is a very loyal friend.
natural_native_use: She remained loyal to the company.
examples: Dogs are known to be loyal animals.
They are loyal supporters of the team.
common_mistakes: Confusing 'loyal' with 'royal' (à¤¶à¤¾à¤¹à¥€).
hindi_confusion_correction: Loyal perfectly translates to वफादार.
formal_informal_note: Highly respected trait in formal and personal contexts.

simple_explanation: Caring only about oneself and not about other people.
daily_life_use: Used when someone doesn't share or thinks only of their own gain.
spoken_use: Don't be so selfish, share your toys.
natural_native_use: It was selfish of him to eat all the pizza.
examples: She is too selfish to care about others.
He made a selfish decision.
common_mistakes: Using 'mean' and 'selfish' interchangeably. 'Mean' is being cruel; 'selfish' is keeping things for yourself.
hindi_confusion_correction: Perfect translation for 'मतलबी'.
formal_informal_note: A strong negative word, use carefully.

hindi_meaning: ख्याल रखने वाला, परवाह करने वाला
simple_explanation: Displaying kindness and concern for others.
daily_life_use: Used to describe loving mothers, nurses, or good friends.
spoken_use: He is a very caring husband.
examples: Thank you for being such a caring friend.
The hospital staff was very caring.
common_mistakes: Saying 'He has caring' instead of 'He is caring'.
hindi_confusion_correction: Comes from the verb 'care' (परवाह करना).

simple_explanation: Demanding that rules concerning behavior are obeyed.
daily_life_use: Used to describe tough teachers, parents, or rules.
spoken_use: My parents are very strict.
natural_native_use: Our teacher is strict but fair.
examples: You need to follow a strict diet.
They are very strict about time.
common_mistakes: Saying 'He is strictly' when describing a person. 'Strictly' is an adverb (strictly prohibited).
hindi_confusion_correction: Translates directly to 'सख्त'.

simple_explanation: Making you smile or laugh.
daily_life_use: Used for jokes, comedians, or friends who make you laugh.
spoken_use: That joke was really funny.
examples: She told a funny story.
I didn't find that funny at all.
common_mistakes: Using 'fun' and 'funny' as the same thing. 'Fun' = enjoyable (a roller coaster). 'Funny' = makes you laugh (a clown).
hindi_confusion_correction: If someone is enjoyable to hang out with, they are 'fun'. If they tell jokes, they are 'funny'.
formal_informal_note: Informal. Can also mean 'strange' (My stomach feels funny).

simple_explanation: Not joking; demanding careful consideration.
daily_life_use: Used for people who don't smile much, or severe situations.
spoken_use: Are you serious?
natural_native_use: He is a very serious student.
examples: This is a serious matter.
She looked very serious during the meeting.
common_mistakes: Saying 'Are you in serious?' instead of 'Are you serious?'.
hindi_confusion_correction: Can mean both 'severe' (serious injury) and 'not joking' (गंभीर इंसान).
